name: workspaces
context: Terraform workspaces -- manage multiple environments (dev, staging, prod) with the same configuration and separate state
info: Terraform workspaces allow a single configuration to manage multiple isolated environments, each with its own state file. Workspaces are ideal for promoting infrastructure through dev, staging, and production using the same HCL code with workspace-aware variable selection. The terraform.workspace interpolation gives access to the current workspace name inside configuration, enabling conditional resource sizing, naming conventions, and environment-specific variable files. Workspaces share the same backend but store state under separate keys. This is lighter weight than full directory-based isolation but less appropriate when environments differ significantly in structure. For CI/CD, workspaces integrate cleanly with pipeline stages: select the workspace, plan, apply.
body:
# Terraform Workspaces

Workspaces let you run the same Terraform configuration against multiple isolated environments, each with its own state file. The default workspace is called `default`. Additional workspaces share the same backend but store state under separate keys, giving each environment its own resource tracking without duplicating configuration.

## Core Commands

```bash
# List all workspaces (* marks current)
terraform workspace list

# Create a new workspace
terraform workspace new dev
terraform workspace new staging
terraform workspace new prod

# Switch to an existing workspace
terraform workspace select staging

# Show current workspace
terraform workspace show

# Delete a workspace (must switch away first, state must be empty)
terraform workspace select default
terraform workspace delete dev
```

## Using terraform.workspace in Configuration

The `terraform.workspace` value is a string containing the current workspace name. Use it to vary resource properties by environment:

```hcl
variable "instance_type" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    dev     = "t3.micro"
    staging = "t3.small"
    prod    = "t3.large"
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type[terraform.workspace]

  tags = {
    Name        = "web-${terraform.workspace}"
    Environment = terraform.workspace
  }
}

# Conditional resource count by environment
resource "aws_instance" "worker" {
  count         = terraform.workspace == "prod" ? 3 : 1
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type[terraform.workspace]

  tags = {
    Name        = "worker-${terraform.workspace}-${count.index}"
    Environment = terraform.workspace
  }
}

# Workspace-aware naming for unique S3 buckets
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" {
  bucket = "myapp-data-${terraform.workspace}"

  tags = {
    Environment = terraform.workspace
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}
```

## Workspace-Specific Variable Files

Use `.tfvars` files per workspace to inject different values:

```
envs/
  dev.tfvars
  staging.tfvars
  prod.tfvars
```

```hcl
# envs/dev.tfvars
db_instance_class  = "db.t3.micro"
min_capacity       = 1
max_capacity       = 2
enable_monitoring   = false

# envs/prod.tfvars
db_instance_class  = "db.r6g.large"
min_capacity       = 3
max_capacity       = 10
enable_monitoring   = true
```

```bash
# Apply with the workspace-matched var file
terraform workspace select staging
terraform plan -var-file="envs/staging.tfvars"
terraform apply -var-file="envs/staging.tfvars"
```

## State Storage by Workspace

With a remote backend, each workspace stores state under a separate key:

```hcl
# S3 backend -- workspace state stored as env:/<workspace>/terraform.tfstate
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "app/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
```

State paths by workspace:
- `default` -- `app/terraform.tfstate`
- `dev` -- `env:/dev/app/terraform.tfstate`
- `staging` -- `env:/staging/app/terraform.tfstate`
- `prod` -- `env:/prod/app/terraform.tfstate`

With Terraform Cloud, each workspace maps to a distinct state in the organization.

## CI/CD Patterns

Workspaces integrate naturally with pipeline stages:

```yaml
# GitHub Actions example
jobs:
  deploy:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        environment: [dev, staging, prod]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init -input=false

      - name: Select Workspace
        run: terraform workspace select ${{ matrix.environment }} || terraform workspace new ${{ matrix.environment }}

      - name: Terraform Plan
        run: terraform plan -var-file="envs/${{ matrix.environment }}.tfvars" -out=tfplan -input=false

      - name: Terraform Apply
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: terraform apply -input=false tfplan
```

```bash
# GitLab CI pattern
# .gitlab-ci.yml
stages:
  - plan
  - apply

.terraform_base: &terraform_base
  image: hashicorp/terraform:1.7
  before_script:
    - terraform init -input=false
    - terraform workspace select $TF_WORKSPACE || terraform workspace new $TF_WORKSPACE

plan:dev:
  <<: *terraform_base
  stage: plan
  variables:
    TF_WORKSPACE: dev
  script:
    - terraform plan -var-file="envs/dev.tfvars" -out=tfplan
  artifacts:
    paths: [tfplan]

apply:dev:
  <<: *terraform_base
  stage: apply
  variables:
    TF_WORKSPACE: dev
  script:
    - terraform apply -input=false tfplan
  when: manual
```

## Workspaces vs Directory-Based Isolation

| Aspect | Workspaces | Directory Isolation |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| Configuration | Single set of .tf files shared | Separate .tf files per environment |
| State | Separate state per workspace, same backend | Separate state files and backends |
| Structural differences | Must handle via conditionals | Each environment can differ freely |
| Complexity | Simpler for identical environments | Better for divergent environments |
| Refactoring | One change affects all environments | Must update each directory |
| Blast radius | Shared code can break all envs | Isolated -- one env cannot affect another |
| Access control | Same backend permissions | Separate backends allow per-env IAM |

**Use workspaces when**: environments are structurally identical and only differ in sizing, counts, and naming.

**Use directory isolation when**: environments have different resources, providers, or significant structural differences. Also preferred when you need per-environment access control at the backend level.

## Best Practices

- Always use `terraform.workspace` in resource names to avoid naming collisions
- Store workspace-specific values in `envs/<workspace>.tfvars` files
- Use the `TF_WORKSPACE` environment variable in CI/CD instead of interactive `workspace select`
- Never store sensitive values in `.tfvars` files committed to version control -- use `TF_VAR_*` environment variables or a secrets manager
- Protect production workspaces with approval gates in CI/CD pipelines
- Delete unused workspaces to reduce state sprawl: `terraform workspace delete <name>`
- Consider Terraform Cloud workspaces for team collaboration with RBAC and run history

sections.title: Core workspace commands
sections.body: `terraform workspace list` shows all workspaces with an asterisk on the current one. `terraform workspace new <name>` creates and switches to a new workspace. `terraform workspace select <name>` switches to an existing workspace. `terraform workspace show` prints the current workspace name. `terraform workspace delete <name>` removes a workspace after its state is empty.

sections.title: Using terraform.workspace in config
sections.body: The `terraform.workspace` expression returns the current workspace name as a string. Use it in resource names (`web-${terraform.workspace}`), map lookups for sizing (`var.instance_type[terraform.workspace]`), conditional counts (`terraform.workspace == "prod" ? 3 : 1`), and tags. This lets one configuration produce different infrastructure per environment.

sections.title: Workspace-specific variable files
sections.body: Store per-environment variables in `envs/<workspace>.tfvars` files. Pass them with `-var-file="envs/${workspace}.tfvars"` during plan and apply. Keep structure identical across files, varying only values. Do not commit secrets to .tfvars files -- use TF_VAR_ environment variables or a secrets manager.

sections.title: State storage per workspace
sections.body: With S3 backend, workspace state is stored at `env:/<workspace>/<key>`. The default workspace uses the key directly. With Terraform Cloud, each workspace is a separate entity in the organization. State locking applies per workspace, so parallel operations on different workspaces do not conflict.

sections.title: CI/CD patterns with workspaces
sections.body: In GitHub Actions, use a matrix strategy over environments and `terraform workspace select $env || terraform workspace new $env`. In GitLab CI, set `TF_WORKSPACE` as a variable per job. Gate production applies behind manual approval. Save plan output as an artifact and apply the exact saved plan.

sections.title: Workspaces vs directory-based isolation
sections.body: Workspaces share one codebase with separate state -- ideal when environments are structurally identical. Directory isolation duplicates config per environment -- better when environments differ in structure, providers, or need separate backend access control. Many teams use workspaces for dev/staging and directory isolation when prod has a fundamentally different topology.

scripts.name: Workspace Manager
scripts.description: Create, select, and deploy to a Terraform workspace with the matching var file
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

WORKSPACE="${WORKSPACE:?ERROR: WORKSPACE is required (e.g. dev, staging, prod)}"
ACTION="${ACTION:-plan}"
VAR_FILE="${VAR_FILE:-envs/${WORKSPACE}.tfvars}"

echo "=== Terraform Workspace Manager ==="
echo "Workspace: $WORKSPACE"
echo "Action:    $ACTION"
echo "Var file:  $VAR_FILE"
echo ""

# Initialize
terraform init -input=false

# Create or select workspace
if terraform workspace list | grep -q "\b${WORKSPACE}\b"; then
  terraform workspace select "$WORKSPACE"
  echo "Selected existing workspace: $WORKSPACE"
else
  terraform workspace new "$WORKSPACE"
  echo "Created new workspace: $WORKSPACE"
fi

# Build var-file flag
VF_FLAG=""
if [ -f "$VAR_FILE" ]; then
  VF_FLAG="-var-file=$VAR_FILE"
  echo "Using var file: $VAR_FILE"
else
  echo "Warning: $VAR_FILE not found, proceeding without it"
fi

case "$ACTION" in
  plan)
    terraform plan $VF_FLAG -out=tfplan -input=false
    echo "\nPlan saved to tfplan. Run with ACTION=apply to apply."
    ;;
  apply)
    if [ -f tfplan ]; then
      terraform apply -input=false tfplan
      rm -f tfplan
    else
      terraform apply $VF_FLAG -input=false -auto-approve
    fi
    echo "\n=== Applied to $WORKSPACE ==="
    ;;
  destroy)
    terraform destroy $VF_FLAG -input=false -auto-approve
    echo "\n=== Destroyed $WORKSPACE ==="
    ;;
  *)
    echo "ERROR: ACTION must be plan, apply, or destroy"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

echo "\n=== Current workspace ==="
terraform workspace show

WORKSPACE.description: Target workspace name (e.g. dev, staging, prod)

ACTION.description: Operation to perform: plan, apply, or destroy

scripts.name: List Workspace States
scripts.description: List all workspaces with their resource counts from state
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

terraform init -input=false > /dev/null 2>&1

CURRENT=$(terraform workspace show)
echo "Workspace Resource Summary"
echo "=========================="

for ws in $(terraform workspace list | sed 's/[* ]//g'); do
  terraform workspace select "$ws" > /dev/null 2>&1
  COUNT=$(terraform state list 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
  MARKER=""
  if [ "$ws" = "$CURRENT" ]; then MARKER=" (current)"; fi
  printf "  %-20s %s resources%s\n" "$ws" "$COUNT" "$MARKER"
done

terraform workspace select "$CURRENT" > /dev/null 2>&1

workspace.description: Workspace name to create or select

depends.reason: Workspace state isolation requires a configured backend

author: dojo-community
